nuggets.text: Thomas Bangalter and Guy-Manuel de Homem-Christo met in a Parisian high school in 1987, sharing a love for '60s and '70s films and music. They first formed an indie rock trio called Darlin' in the early 90s, where Guy-Manuel played guitar. A negative review famously called their music "a daft punky thrash," which ironically inspired their new name when they pivoted to electronic music after discovering a club in 1992.
nuggets.headline: Before they donned their iconic robot helmets, Daft Punk were actually in a rock band!

nuggets.text: The repetitive vocal hook, which says "around the world" exactly 144 times in the album version, is actually a key part of the song's brilliance and even the iconic music video's concept. Director Michel Gondry specifically designed the video, featuring different groups of characters (robots, athletes, disco girls, skeletons, and mummies) dancing to represent each instrument, to visually echo this repetitive, cyclical nature of the lyrics and the music's structure.
nuggets.headline: The seemingly simple lyrics of 'Around the World' are repeated 144 times, but they're far from meaningless!

nuggets.text: Cassius, another pioneering French electronic duo featuring Philippe Zdar and Boombass, released their album '1999' in the same era as Daft Punk's 'Homework,' and it shares that irresistible French Touch sound. It's packed with funky basslines, filtered disco samples, and a joyful, dancefloor-ready vibe that will feel instantly familiar and fantastic if you loved this track.
nuggets.headline: If the infectious groove of 'Around the World' has you hooked, you absolutely need to check out Cassius's debut album, '1999'!

nuggets.text: Thomas Bangalter once revealed that their very first electronic music gig as Daft Punk, at a club in Marseille in 1994 or 1995, was quite nerve-wracking. He shared that his bandmate, Guy-Manuel de Homem-Christo, was so overwhelmed he spent almost half the show adjusting machines from underneath the table!
nuggets.headline: Can you imagine Daft Punk getting nervous for their first live show, with one of them literally hiding under a table?

nuggets.text: Director Michel Gondry had a brilliant concept for the 'Around the World' video: the robots embody the vocals, the athletes represent the bassline, the disco girls are the keyboards, the skeletons are the guitars, and the mummies perform the drums. Daft Punk themselves described making the track as 'like making a Chic record with a talk box and just playing the bass on the synthesizer.'
nuggets.headline: The iconic music video for 'Around the World' isn't just cool choreography—each group of dancers actually represents a different instrument in the song!

nuggets.text: Alan Braxe is a French house pioneer with a deep connection to Daft Punk—Thomas Bangalter even released Braxe's debut single 'Vertigo' on his Roulé label. His compilation 'The Upper Cuts' is a fantastic entry point, brimming with smooth, funky, and impeccably produced tracks that capture that quintessential 'French Touch' sound.
nuggets.headline: If that signature Daft Punk groove makes your head nod, you absolutely have to dive into Alan Braxe's 'The Upper Cuts' compilation.

nuggets.text: Thomas Bangalter, one half of Daft Punk, recently opened up about the duo's split and the philosophy behind their robot identities, stating he was "terrified of the nature of the relationship between the machines and ourselves." He emphasized that Daft Punk always aimed to use machines to express something profoundly human, rather than simply embracing technology for its own sake.
nuggets.headline: Beyond the helmets, one half of Daft Punk recently revealed a surprising reason behind the duo's enigmatic robot personas, deeply rooted in a modern fear.

nuggets.text: Directed by the visionary Michel Gondry, the 'Around the World' music video features five groups of dancers – robots, athletes, disco girls, skeletons, and mummies – each symbolizing a different instrument in the song. Gondry deliberately chose to avoid quick cuts and fast editing, a common practice in music videos, to let choreographer Blanca Li's intricate work truly shine and emphasize the choreography itself.
nuggets.headline: The iconic music video for 'Around the World' features distinct groups of dancers, but the director had a very specific, almost rebellious, vision for how their intricate choreography should be filmed.

nuggets.text: For that quintessential *Homework*-era Daft Punk vibe, you should definitely dive into Le Knight Club, a project Guy-Manuel de Homem-Christo formed with Eric Chedeville. Their tracks often lean into that direct, old-school disco and funk influence, feeling like a natural extension of Daft Punk's early sound. Start with tracks like 'Holiday On Ice' or 'Santa Claus' to get a feel for their irresistible grooves.
nuggets.headline: If the groovy, filtered sounds of 'Around the World' scratch just the right itch, you absolutely need to check out the side project of Daft Punk's very own Guy-Manuel de Homem-Christo!

trackStory: "Around the World" was released in 1997 as the second single from Daft Punk's debut album, "Homework." The track is famous for its hypnotic, repetitive structure, featuring the phrase "around the world" repeated 144 times in the album version. Its catchy bassline, robotic vocals, and Michel Gondry's iconic music video helped make it a global club hit and a landmark in electronic music, symbolizing global connectivity and the universal language of dance.
artistSummary: Daft Punk was a French electronic music duo known for their groundbreaking blend of house, disco, and funk, often appearing as iconic robots. They profoundly influenced electronic dance music with their innovative sound and distinct visual identity.
